7: A direct revelation: 'You are my Son; today I have begotten you.' The royal Psalm 2 reaches its Christological fullness in the resurrection. Paul at Pisidian Antioch (Acts 13:33): God raised Jesus, 'as it is written in the second Psalm, You are my Son, today I have begotten you.' The 'today' of divine fatherhood is not the eternal generation alone but the specific day of the resurrection-enthronement, when the Son's identity is publicly declared. Every application of Psalms 2:7 in the NT (baptism, transfiguration, resurrection) marks a moment when the Father publicly declares the Son's identity for the community to hear.
1: A direct fulfillment: 'why do the nations rage, and the peoples plot in vain?' Acts 4:25-26 quotes this verse explicitly as fulfilled in the conspiracy against Jesus: the apostles pray, 'Sovereign Lord... you spoke by the Holy Spirit through the mouth of our father David: why did the Gentiles rage, and the peoples plot in vain?' The nations and peoples of Ps 2 are identified as Herod, Pontius Pilate, the Gentiles, and the people of Israel — all gathered against Jesus (Acts 4:27). The rage of the nations is the crucifixion.
2: A direct fulfillment: 'the kings of the earth set themselves, and the rulers take counsel together, against the LORD and against his Anointed' — meshicho (his Anointed, his Messiah). The Davidic king of Ps 2 is the Anointed One by title and function; the NT reads this title as referring ultimately to Jesus (Acts 4:26: 'against the Lord and against his Christ'). The coalition that crucified Jesus — Roman power, Herodian client king, Jewish religious leadership — is the fulfillment of the multi-party conspiracy Ps 2 describes.
3: A revelation of God: 'let us burst their bonds apart and cast away their cords from us' — the rulers' ambition to free themselves from YHWH's authority. The crucifixion was humanity's definitive attempt to throw off divine governance by killing the divine Son. The claim 'we have no king but Caesar' (John 19:15) is the explicit assertion of this rejection. Yet the attempt to break the cords of covenant becomes the very means by which Christ binds those who trust him (Matthew 11:29-30: 'my yoke is easy').
4: A revelation of God: 'he who sits in the heavens laughs; the Lord holds them in derision.' The divine laughter at the conspiracy of the powerful is not cruelty but the perception of futility: the plot to overthrow the Creator's Son cannot succeed. The resurrection vindicates the divine laughter — the cross that the conspirators thought final was the precise instrument of their own defeat (1 Corinthians 2:8: 'if they had understood, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory').
5: A revelation of God: 'then he will speak to them in his wrath and terrify them in his fury.' The divine speech in judgment contrasts with the nations' conspiring speech. The final judgment that Ps 2 sets in the future is what Christ's return inaugurates (Revelation 19:11-16): the one who was judged by the nations comes as judge. The wrath that Ps 2 announces is what Christ absorbs at the cross (Romans 5:9; 1 Thessalonians 1:10) so that those in him are not subject to it.
6: A direct connection: 'as for me, I have set my King on Zion, my holy hill.' The divine installation of the Davidic king on Zion is what the NT interprets as Christ's resurrection and ascension. Acts 2:36 (Peter at Pentecost): 'God has made him both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified.' The installation of YHWH's King on the holy hill is not Solomon's coronation but the resurrection enthronement of Jesus at the Father's right hand (Psalms 110:1; Acts 2:33-34).
8: A direct connection: 'ask of me, and I will make the nations your heritage, and the ends of the earth your possession.' The Father's promise to the Son in Psalms 2:8 is what Jesus claims in the Great Commission (Matthew 28:18-20: 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Go therefore and make disciples of all nations'). The nations as the Son's inheritance is the ground of the universal mission: the Father has already given them; the mission gathers what belongs to the Son.
9: A direct connection: 'you shall break them with a rod of iron and dash them in pieces like a potter's vessel.' Revelation 2:27, Psalms 12:5, and 19:15 all apply this verse to Christ. In Revelation 19:15 the rider on the white horse (Christ) 'will rule the nations with a rod of iron.' The iron scepter is both the authority of the enthroned Christ and the instrument of eschatological judgment on all who refuse the Son.
10: A theme: 'now therefore, O kings, be wise; be warned, O rulers of the earth.' The summons to the kings anticipates the universal call of the gospel: all rulers, all power-holders, are called to submit to the Son. The 'wisdom' (haskilah) that kings need is the wisdom that acknowledges Christ's lordship — the fear of the Lord that Psalms 1:6 and Psalms 111:10 describe as wisdom's beginning.
11: A theme: 'serve the LORD with fear, and rejoice with trembling.' The paradox of trembling-joy — fear and gladness combined — is the posture that Christ's cross and resurrection create in those who receive him. The NT calls it 'reverent fear' (Philippians 2:12: 'work out your salvation with fear and trembling') precisely because the grace that saves is also the holiness that terrifies. The resurrection-joy that comes with acknowledging the Son includes the trembling of those who understand what was at stake.
12: A direct connection: 'kiss the Son, lest he be angry, and you perish in the way' — 'blessed are all who take refuge in him.' The 'kiss' as covenant submission and loyalty: in the ANE, kissing the feet of the conquering king was the act of submission. The warning and the beatitude together frame the gospel's structure: the Son is both the one who judges and the one in whom refuge is found. The last word of Ps 2 (ashre, blessed) echoes the first word of Ps 1 (ashre, blessed): the entire framework of the blessed life is defined by relation to the Son.
